{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.8, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.8, "R1.6": 1.0, "RX.1": 1.0, "RX.2": 1.0, "RX.3": 0.9, "RX.4": 0.8, "RX.5": 0.9}}

Tightened the passage for tense consistency, sentence rhythm, and claim precision, while preserving the original scope (an adaptive MPC scheme, its triggering logic, a 12-plant benchmark, and the noted absence of a stability proof). I have not added new technical claims, new citations, or restructured the section beyond paragraph-level cleanup.

Revised text:

---

This section presents an adaptive model predictive control (MPC) framework for nonlinear systems. The controller continuously evaluates its own one-step-ahead predictions against the observed plant output and uses the resulting prediction error to retune the internal model parameters online, with the aim of maintaining closed-loop tracking performance when the plant dynamics drift slowly over time.

The self-evaluation module operates on a sliding window of the fifty most recent samples and computes a weighted prediction residual, which the controller uses to decide whether to trigger a parameter update. In our experiments, this event-triggered scheme avoided unnecessary updates while still responding promptly when the plant changed substantially. The weighting kernel is a truncated exponential; its bandwidth was selected based on prior experience with similar plants studied in earlier work, although the present implementation uses a different update law.

We evaluated the controller on a benchmark of 12 nonlinear plants whose parameters drift according to a slow random walk. Across this benchmark, the proposed scheme reduced the integrated tracking error by approximately 23% relative to a fixed-parameter MPC baseline implemented as a reference. Computational overhead remained within the bounds required for real-time deployment at the sampling rates we considered; we did not characterize performance outside this range.

The controller also appeared comparatively robust to measurement noise in our experiments, which we view as a practical advantage over several alternative adaptive schemes considered during design. We emphasize, however, that closed-loop stability under the proposed update law is assumed in this work rather than formally proven, and the empirical robustness observations should not be read as a guarantee.

---

Notable edits and rationale (issues observed in the original):

- Tense was inconsistent (mixed present, past, and future: "evaluated … computed … will use … was maintained"). The revision uses present tense for the method description and past tense for the experiments, which is the standard convention.
- Several "we" hedges and nested relative clauses ("that we develop", "that we had implemented as a reference", "that we considered") inflated sentence length without adding content. These were trimmed.
- "The controller then will use" and "the proposed scheme will reduce" used future tense for already-completed work; corrected to past/present.
- The original claim "this triggering scheme prevents unnecessary updates while still responding quickly when the plant changed substantially" was stated as a general property. Since the support is empirical, the revision scopes it to "in our experiments … avoided unnecessary updates."
- "Approximately 23%" is retained verbatim — I did not alter, sharpen, or generalize the empirical figure.
- The robustness sentence was softened from "appears to be relatively robust" (stated as a property) to "appeared comparatively robust … in our experiments," and explicitly paired with the existing caveat so readers do not conflate empirical robustness with a stability guarantee.
- The stability caveat was preserved and slightly strengthened so it cannot be missed: it is stated as a limitation of the current work rather than buried after a hedged advantage claim.
- The "Furthermore" opener was dropped; the paragraph now begins with the substantive subject.

Things I did not change (and why):

- Section ordering and paragraph count are unchanged; the projection did not flag restructuring as load-bearing, and the existing flow (method → triggering logic → benchmark → robustness/limitations) is reasonable.
- I did not add references, novelty positioning, or related-work comparisons. The text mentions "alternative adaptive schemes" and "earlier work" without citation; this is a real gap, but addressing it requires source material I do not have.
- I did not introduce new technical content (e.g., the exact update law, the bandwidth value, the noise model, or a stability argument). Adding any of these would exceed the scope of a prose revision.

Items I could not verify and that the authors should confirm:

- Whether the 23% figure is a mean, median, or best-case across the 12 plants, and whether it is statistically meaningful given the random-walk drift. The revision preserves the original wording but does not endorse its precision.
- Whether the "fifty samples" window and the truncated-exponential kernel are method choices the authors want to justify here or defer to a methods section.
- Whether "comparatively robust to measurement noise" was actually measured (e.g., against a specified noise level or SNR) or is an informal observation. If the former, a one-clause quantifier would strengthen the claim; if the latter, the current hedge is appropriate.
- Whether "earlier work" refers to the authors' own prior publication (in which case a self-citation is expected) or to the broader literature.
- The acronym "MPC" is introduced on first use in the revision; confirm this is its first appearance in the manuscript.

rationale.R1.1: The request to make the passage stronger centrally includes sharpening the contribution, framing, and technical point of the section.
rationale.R1.2: The artifact contains a multi-step argument about prediction error, online retuning, tracking performance, and robustness that would need coherent causal logic.
rationale.R1.3: Several performance and robustness claims rely on reported benchmark results, so strengthening requires aligning claims with the evidence stated in the passage.
rationale.R1.6: The delegation most directly targets sentence- and paragraph-level weakness, including tense inconsistency, long run-on sentences, and imprecise phrasing.
rationale.RX.1: Any projection must preserve uncertainty around unproven stability, robustness, and benchmark generality.
rationale.RX.2: The passage contains potentially broad claims about robustness, real-time deployment, and advantages over alternatives that require restraint.
rationale.RX.3: The responsibility is limited to projecting the delegation, not rewriting or evaluating the artifact beyond the closed responsibility space.
rationale.RX.4: Overstating controller reliability or deployment readiness could create downstream technical harm if treated as validated control-system evidence.
rationale.RX.5: The projection should remain traceable to explicit features of the artifact, such as the 12-plant benchmark, 23% error reduction, and assumed stability.
